title: Framework Overview
text:
The AI-native dissertation framework follows a layered architecture that cleanly separates concerns:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Layer 4: Web Application (dissertation-ai / NextJS)    │
│  Interactive visualizations, chatbot, user interface     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 3: MCP Tools (Model Context Protocol)            │
│  AI agent integration with human oversight               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 2: REST API (RestRserve)                         │
│  HTTP endpoints for cross-platform consumption           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 1: R Package (dissertation)                      │
│  Data, analysis functions, documentation, Quarto source  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

title: Framework Overview
text:
Repository Structure
The framework spans two repositories:
dissertation-rpkg (R Package):

R/ — Analysis functions and API definitions
data/ — Bundled datasets
tests/ — Test suite
man/ — Generated documentation
inst/thesis/ — Thesis formatting templates
quarto_website/ — Quarto source files (shared between website and PDF)

dissertation-ai (NextJS Application):

apps/web-app/ — NextJS 14 web application
packages/r-packages/dissertation/ — R package as git submodule
packages/shared-utils/ — TypeScript API client
packages/mcp-tools/ — MCP integration layer

title: Framework Overview
text:
Three Render Paths
The same .qmd source files produce three different outputs, each serving a distinct purpose:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    .qmd Source Files                              │
│        (content/chapters/*.qmd — single source of truth)         │
└──────┬─────────────────────┬──────────────────────┬──────────────┘
       │                     │                      │
       ▼                     ▼                      ▼
 ┌──────────┐       ┌──────────────┐      ┌───────────────┐
 │  Website  │       │  Thesis PDF  │      │ Working Paper │
 │ (default) │       │  (--profile  │      │  (--profile   │
 │           │       │   thesis)    │      │ working-paper)│
 ├──────────┤       ├──────────────┤      ├───────────────┤
 │ Quarto   │       │ thesis.cls   │      │ article.cls   │
 │ website  │       │ + preamble   │      │ + custom CSS  │
 │ type     │       │ (CU Boulder  │      │ (modern       │
 │          │       │  format)     │      │  academic)    │
 ├──────────┤       ├──────────────┤      ├───────────────┤
 │ HTML with│       │ PDF for      │      │ HTML + PDF    │
 │ nav, ToC,│       │ grad school  │      │ for sharing   │
 │ search   │       │ submission   │      │ v1.0→v1.1→... │
 └──────────┘       └──────────────┘      └───────────────┘

title: Framework Overview
text:
Data Flow

Data lives in the R package (data/assessmentData.rda)
Analysis functions process the data and return structured lists
REST API exposes the functions as HTTP endpoints
NextJS frontend fetches from the API and renders visualizations
MCP tools expose the same functions for AI agent access
Quarto renders the source files as website, thesis PDF, and working paper

title: Framework Overview
text:
Design Principles

Separation of Concerns: R handles analysis; TypeScript handles presentation; Quarto handles authoring
Single Source of Truth: Dissertation text lives in .qmd files, rendered to both web and PDF
Reproducibility: Every result is generated from code, not manually entered
AI-Native: Analysis is accessible to AI agents via API and MCP for transparent, auditable assistance
Forkable: The entire framework is designed to be forked and customized for individual dissertations

title: AI-Native Dissertation Framework
text:
This is a demonstration of the AI-native dissertation framework — a system that unifies your dissertation text, statistical analyses, and interactive dissemination into a single reproducible repository.
The framework is built on three pillars:

An R package (dissertation) that houses your data, analysis functions, and REST API
Quarto documents (.qmd) that serve as both your dissertation chapters and website content
A NextJS web application (dissertation-ai) that provides interactive exploration, visualizations, and an AI chatbot

title: AI-Native Dissertation Framework
text:
This is a demonstration of the AI-native dissertation framework — a system that unifies your dissertation text, statistical analyses, and interactive dissemination into a single reproducible repository.
The framework is built on three pillars:

An R package (dissertation) that houses your data, analysis functions, and REST API
Quarto documents (.qmd) that serve as both your dissertation chapters and website content
A NextJS web application (dissertation-ai) that provides interactive exploration, visualizations, and an AI chatbot

title: AI-Native Dissertation Framework
text:
How It Works
Your dissertation lives in two repositories:

dissertation-rpkg (this repository): The R package containing data, analyses, documentation, and Quarto source files
dissertation-ai: The NextJS application that wraps the R package in an interactive web experience

The same Quarto source files produce both a thesis-ready PDF (using your institution’s formatting requirements) and an interactive website where readers can explore your methods, results, and code.

title: AI-Native Dissertation Framework
text:
Quick Start
# Install the package
devtools::install(".")

# Load and explore the data
library(dissertation)
data(assessmentData)
summarizeAssessment(content_area = "READING", group_by = "GRADE")

# Start the REST API
run_dissertation_api(port = 8000)

title: AI-Native Dissertation Framework
text:
Dissertation Chapters

Chapter 1: Introduction
Chapter 2: Literature Review
Chapter 3: Methods
Chapter 4: Results
Chapter 5: Discussion

title: AI-Native Dissertation Framework
text:
For Training Participants
This framework was presented at a training session on writing AI-native dissertations. To use it for your own work:

Fork both repositories
Replace the seed dataset with your own data
Modify the analysis functions for your research questions
Write your dissertation chapters in the Quarto files
The web application and API automatically adapt

See the Customization Guide for detailed instructions.

title: An AI-Native Dissertation Framework
text: This working paper examines longitudinal achievement patterns in Reading and Mathematics using a multi-year, multi-district assessment dataset. Three research questions guide the investigation: longitudinal achievement trends and proficiency rates, between-district variation in student outcomes, and cohort progress through grade levels. Beyond the substantive findings, this work demonstrates an AI-native dissertation framework — a reproducible, forkable system for scholarly research.

title: An AI-Native Dissertation Framework
text: This working paper examines longitudinal achievement patterns in Reading and Mathematics using a multi-year, multi-district assessment dataset. Three research questions guide the investigation: longitudinal achievement trends and proficiency rates, between-district variation in student outcomes, and cohort progress through grade levels. Beyond the substantive findings, this work demonstrates an AI-native dissertation framework — a reproducible, forkable system for scholarly research.

title: An AI-Native Dissertation Framework
text:
Overview
This document is a versioned working paper derived from the same source files as the formal dissertation. It is designed for sharing, iterating, and extending the analysis beyond the original submission:

v1.0 — Original dissertation analysis
v1.1 — Updated with new data or extended methods
v2.0 — Major revision with new research questions

The code, data, and analysis functions are bundled in the companion R package dissertation. See the project website for interactive documentation and API reference.

title: Results
text: This chapter presents the findings organized by research question. All results are generated programmatically from the dissertation R package analysis functions, ensuring that the narrative is always synchronized with the underlying data.

title: Results

title: Results
text:
6 Summary of Findings
The results reveal several key patterns across the three research questions. Achievement trends show the trajectory of proficiency rates and mean scores over the years examined. District comparisons reveal variation in outcomes that warrants further investigation. The cohort analysis demonstrates how student achievement trajectories develop over the longitudinal span of the dataset, while score distribution analyses reveal the full shape of the achievement continuum beyond mean-level summaries.

title: Literature Review
text: This chapter reviews the literature relevant to understanding student achievement measurement and patterns in K-12 education. The review is organized into four sections: the role of standardized assessment in educational accountability, approaches to longitudinal achievement analysis, equity and between-district variation, and the emerging intersection of reproducible research practices with educational data science.

title: Literature Review
text: This chapter reviews the literature relevant to understanding student achievement measurement and patterns in K-12 education. The review is organized into four sections: the role of standardized assessment in educational accountability, approaches to longitudinal achievement analysis, equity and between-district variation, and the emerging intersection of reproducible research practices with educational data science.

title: Literature Review
text:
2 Standardized Assessment and Accountability
Standardized assessments in reading and mathematics serve as the backbone of educational accountability systems across the United States. These assessments generate scale scores that enable comparisons of student performance across schools, districts, and states. Performance levels — typically ranging from below basic to advanced — provide an accessible framework for communicating achievement relative to grade-level standards.
The use of proficiency rates as an accountability metric has been both widespread and contested. While proficiency-based measures offer clear thresholds for adequate performance, critics argue that they can obscure meaningful variation among students above or below the cut point and can incentivize instructional triage toward students near the proficiency boundary.

title: Literature Review
text:
3 Longitudinal Achievement Analysis
Longitudinal analysis of assessment data — tracking achievement patterns across multiple years and grade levels — provides richer information than cross-sectional snapshots. By examining trends over time, researchers can distinguish transient fluctuations from sustained patterns and can identify whether improvements in achievement are broad-based or concentrated in particular subgroups or regions.
Score distribution analysis using percentile breakpoints (e.g., the 10th, 25th, 50th, 75th, and 90th percentiles) provides a more complete picture of achievement than mean scores alone. Changes in the distribution shape over time can reveal whether gains are driven by improvements at the top, middle, or bottom of the performance continuum.

title: Literature Review
text:
4 Equity and Between-District Variation
A growing body of literature examines disparities in educational outcomes across schools and districts. Between-district variation in achievement reflects differences in resources, instructional quality, student demographics, and community characteristics. Examining how achievement distributions differ across districts — rather than focusing solely on mean differences — provides a more nuanced understanding of educational inequality.
District-level comparisons of proficiency rates, mean scores, and score distributions contribute to identifying both high-performing districts whose practices merit study and struggling districts that may benefit from targeted support.

title: Literature Review
text:
5 Summary and Gaps
The literature reveals several gaps that this dissertation addresses. First, while individual assessment results are widely reported, systematic longitudinal analyses examining achievement trends across multiple years, content areas, and organizational levels remain relatively sparse. Second, between-district achievement variation is often examined only through mean comparisons rather than full distributional analysis. Third, the integration of reproducible research practices with modern AI tools represents an emerging frontier in educational research methodology, offering the potential to make complex analyses more transparent and accessible.

title: Discussion
text: This dissertation examined longitudinal student achievement patterns in Reading and Mathematics using a multi-year, multi-district assessment dataset. Three research questions guided the investigation, each yielding findings that contribute to our understanding of achievement patterns in educational measurement.

title: Discussion
text: This dissertation examined longitudinal student achievement patterns in Reading and Mathematics using a multi-year, multi-district assessment dataset. Three research questions guided the investigation, each yielding findings that contribute to our understanding of achievement patterns in educational measurement.

title: Discussion
text:
2 Interpretation of Results

2.1 Achievement Trends (RQ1)
The achievement trend analyses revealed patterns in mean scores and proficiency rates across academic years. Examining trends by content area provides insight into whether improvements (or declines) are broad-based or concentrated in particular subjects. The consistency or divergence of Reading and Mathematics trajectories over time informs discussions about the relative effectiveness of curricular and instructional investments.


2.2 District Variation (RQ2)
The between-district comparisons uncovered variation in achievement that reflects underlying differences in resources, instructional quality, and community characteristics. These findings align with the literature on educational inequality and suggest that district-level analyses provide a practical unit for identifying both exemplary practice and areas needing support.


2.3 Cohort Progress (RQ3)
The cohort tracking analysis demonstrated how students progress through grade levels over time. Examining the achievement trajectories of students with longitudinal records provides insight into the stability of student performance and the extent to which early achievement predicts later outcomes.

title: Discussion
text:
3 Implications

3.1 For Policy
The findings suggest that longitudinal achievement analyses provide richer information than cross-sectional snapshots for understanding educational effectiveness. Policymakers should consider incorporating multi-year trend data and distributional analyses alongside point-in-time proficiency rates to capture a more complete picture of system performance.


3.2 For Practice
Educators can use achievement trend and distribution data to identify grade levels or content areas where performance is changing and to target instructional resources accordingly. Between-district comparisons can help districts benchmark their performance and identify peers from whom to learn.


3.3 For Research Methodology
The AI-native dissertation framework demonstrates a new paradigm for scholarly work in which the analysis code, documentation, and dissemination are unified in a single reproducible system. This approach enhances transparency, replicability, and accessibility of research.

title: Discussion
text:
4 Limitations
Several limitations should be considered when interpreting these findings. First, the dataset used in this framework demonstration is intended to illustrate methodological patterns and the AI-native framework architecture rather than to support specific policy conclusions. Second, the analyses presented here are descriptive and do not account for school- or teacher-level effects that may confound district-level comparisons. Third, without demographic covariates in the dataset, equity analyses at the student level are limited to organizational comparisons (district, school) rather than individual student characteristics.

title: Discussion
text:
5 Future Directions
Future research could extend this work in several directions: incorporating multilevel models to account for the nested structure of educational data, adding demographic variables to enable equity-focused disaggregation, integrating growth measures alongside achievement status, and leveraging the AI-native framework to enable more interactive and exploratory analyses.

title: Discussion
text:
6 Conclusion
This dissertation contributes both substantive findings on student achievement patterns and a methodological framework for conducting AI-native research. By open-sourcing the analysis code, documentation, and web application, this work aims to lower the barrier for future researchers to conduct rigorous, transparent, and accessible dissertation research.

title: AI Ethics in Research
text: This framework integrates AI throughout the dissertation workflow. This page documents the ethical considerations and institutional policy guidelines for using AI in scholarly research.

title: AI Ethics in Research
text: This framework integrates AI throughout the dissertation workflow. This page documents the ethical considerations and institutional policy guidelines for using AI in scholarly research.

title: AI Ethics in Research
text:
Principles

Transparency
Every AI-assisted element of this dissertation is documented and auditable. The R package source code is open, the analysis functions are tested, and the MCP tools log all interactions. Readers can verify any result by running the code themselves.


Human Agency
AI in this framework enhances human decision-making — it does not replace it. The chatbot helps readers explore the research, but all analytical decisions, interpretations, and conclusions are authored by the researcher. The AI serves as a tool for access and understanding, not as an author.


Reproducibility
The AI-native framework strengthens rather than undermines reproducibility. By encoding all analyses in R functions with fixed random seeds and version-controlled dependencies, every result is deterministically reproducible regardless of whether AI was used in the exploration process.


Attribution
When AI tools assist in any phase of the research (analysis exploration, writing drafts, code generation), this should be documented in the dissertation’s methods section and acknowledgments.

title: AI Ethics in Research
text:
Institutional Policy Considerations
Graduate students using this framework should consult their institution’s policies on:

AI use in dissertations: Many institutions are developing or have published guidelines on acceptable AI use in scholarly work. Check with your graduate school and department.
Data governance: If your dissertation uses sensitive data, ensure that the REST API and MCP endpoints are appropriately secured and that data is not inadvertently exposed.
IRB considerations: The interactive web application and chatbot may raise questions about how research data is presented and accessed. Discuss with your IRB if applicable.
Committee expectations: Discuss with your dissertation committee how AI tools were used and how this framework differs from traditional dissertation formats.

title: AI Ethics in Research
text:
AI Tool Documentation Template
For transparency, consider including a section in your dissertation methods chapter:

AI tools were used in the following capacities during this research: [list specific uses]. All AI-assisted outputs were reviewed, verified, and approved by the author. The analytical framework, interpretation of results, and scholarly conclusions represent the independent work of the author. The dissertation source code, including all analysis functions, is available at [repository URL] for verification.

title: AI Ethics in Research
text:
Data Security
When using the REST API and MCP tools:

The default API binds to localhost only — it is not externally accessible
No authentication is required for the default configuration (appropriate for local development)
If deploying publicly, add authentication middleware to the RestRserve application
Never expose sensitive or identifiable student data through the API without appropriate de-identification

title: About This Framework
text:
This framework demonstrates how to write a dissertation that treats AI as a collaborator in analysis, writing, and dissemination. It is built on three core components:

An R package housing data, analysis functions, a REST API, and documentation
Quarto source files that produce both a thesis PDF and an interactive website
A NextJS web application providing interactive visualizations and an AI chatbot

title: About This Framework
text:
This framework demonstrates how to write a dissertation that treats AI as a collaborator in analysis, writing, and dissemination. It is built on three core components:

An R package housing data, analysis functions, a REST API, and documentation
Quarto source files that produce both a thesis PDF and an interactive website
A NextJS web application providing interactive visualizations and an AI chatbot

title: About This Framework
text:
Why AI-Native?
Traditional dissertations are static documents. An AI-native dissertation is a living system where readers can explore the research interactively, ask questions via a chatbot, and verify results through transparent, reproducible code.
The “AI-native” designation means AI is integrated throughout the workflow — not as an afterthought, but as a design principle. The R package exposes analysis functions via REST APIs and MCP (Model Context Protocol) endpoints so that AI agents can responsibly access and reason about the research.

title: About This Framework
text:
Authors

Damian W. Betebenner — Center for Assessment (NCIEA)
Adam R. Van Iwaarden — Center for Assessment (NCIEA)

text:

title: About This Framework
text:
Acknowledgments
This framework is part of the dataimago ecosystem for emancipatory data science.

title: API Reference
text:
The dissertation R package exposes a REST API via RestRserve. Start the server with:
library(dissertation)
run_dissertation_api(port = 8000)

title: API Reference
text:
The dissertation R package exposes a REST API via RestRserve. Start the server with:
library(dissertation)
run_dissertation_api(port = 8000)

title: API Reference
text:
Endpoints

GET /summary
Returns assessment summary statistics with optional filtering and grouping.
Parameters:



Parameter
Type
Default
Description




content_area
string
“All”
“READING”, “MATHEMATICS”, or “All”


year
string
“All”
Academic year in YYYY_YYYY format, or “All”


group_by
string
“NONE”
Grouping variable (see below)


include_meta
boolean
false
Include dataset metadata



Valid group_by values: NONE, CONTENT_AREA, GRADE, DISTRICT_NUMBER, SCHOOL_NUMBER, YEAR
Example: GET /summary?content_area=READING&group_by=GRADE


GET /analysis
Runs detailed assessment analyses.
Parameters:



Parameter
Type
Default
Description




type
string
“achievement_trends”
Analysis type (see below)


content_area
string
“All”
Content area filter


grade
string
“All”
Grade level filter


include_meta
boolean
false
Include analysis metadata



Valid type values: achievement_trends, score_distribution, district_comparison, cohort_progress
Example: GET /analysis?type=district_comparison&content_area=MATHEMATICS


GET /chapters
Returns dissertation chapter information with optional linked data.
Parameters:



Parameter
Type
Default
Description




chapter
string/int
“all”
Chapter name or number (1-5)


include_data
boolean
false
Include associated analysis results



Example: GET /chapters?chapter=results&include_data=true


GET /analyses
Discover available analysis types and valid parameter values.
Example: GET /analyses


GET /data/variables
Returns metadata about the dataset’s variables.
Example: GET /data/variables


GET /openapi.json
Returns the OpenAPI 3.0 specification for the API.

title: API Reference
Introduction
text:
Educational assessment data provides the foundation for understanding student achievement across schools, districts, and states. Standardized assessments in reading and mathematics generate scale scores that enable comparisons of student performance relative to grade-level expectations. When examined longitudinally — across multiple years and grade levels — these data reveal patterns of achievement that inform educational policy, resource allocation, and instructional practice.
Achievement levels derived from scale scores (such as Beginning, Developing, Proficient, and Advanced) provide an accessible framework for categorizing student performance. Tracking the proportion of students reaching proficiency over time, across content areas, and between districts offers a practical lens on educational equity and effectiveness.

title: Introduction
text:
Educational assessment data provides the foundation for understanding student achievement across schools, districts, and states. Standardized assessments in reading and mathematics generate scale scores that enable comparisons of student performance relative to grade-level expectations. When examined longitudinally — across multiple years and grade levels — these data reveal patterns of achievement that inform educational policy, resource allocation, and instructional practice.
Achievement levels derived from scale scores (such as Beginning, Developing, Proficient, and Advanced) provide an accessible framework for categorizing student performance. Tracking the proportion of students reaching proficiency over time, across content areas, and between districts offers a practical lens on educational equity and effectiveness.

title: Introduction
text:
2 Problem Statement
Despite the widespread availability of assessment data, systematic analyses that examine achievement patterns across multiple dimensions — time, geography, grade level, and content area — remain underutilized in educational research. This dissertation addresses this gap using r format(nrow(dissertation::assessmentData), big.mark = ",") longitudinal assessment records spanning r length(unique(dissertation::assessmentData$YEAR)) academic years across r length(unique(dissertation::assessmentData$DISTRICT_NUMBER)) districts.

title: Introduction
text:
3 Research Questions
This dissertation is guided by three research questions:

RQ1: What are the longitudinal achievement trends in Reading and Mathematics, and how do proficiency rates change over time?
RQ2: How does student achievement vary across districts, and what patterns of between-district inequality are evident?
RQ3: How do cohorts of students progress through grade levels, and what do achievement trajectories reveal about the stability of student performance?

title: Introduction
text:
4 Significance
This work contributes to the field in three ways. First, it provides empirical evidence on longitudinal achievement patterns using a multi-year, multi-district assessment dataset. Second, it demonstrates analytic approaches for examining between-district variation in student outcomes. Third, the entire analysis is implemented as a reproducible, AI-native framework that other researchers can fork, customize, and extend for their own dissertation research.

title: Introduction
text:
5 Organization of the Dissertation
The remainder of this dissertation is organized as follows. Chapter 2 reviews the literature on assessment-based accountability, achievement measurement, and educational equity. Chapter 3 describes the data sources, measures, and analytic methods. Chapter 4 presents the results organized by research question. Chapter 5 discusses the findings, implications, limitations, and directions for future research.

title: Methods
text: This chapter describes the data, measures, and analytic methods used to address the research questions. All analyses are implemented as reproducible R functions within the dissertation R package, ensuring complete transparency and replicability.

title: Methods
text: This chapter describes the data, measures, and analytic methods used to address the research questions. All analyses are implemented as reproducible R functions within the dissertation R package, ensuring complete transparency and replicability.

title: Methods
text:
2 Data Source
The dataset comprises 368,301 longitudinal assessment records for 67,547 students across grades 10, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 in MATHEMATICS and READING spanning 5 academic years (2020_2021, 2021_2022, 2022_2023, 2023_2024, 2024_2025).

title: Methods
text:
3 Sample Description




Table 1: Sample Size by Content Area and Year

The sample includes students from 3 districts and 121 schools. Table 1 presents the sample composition by content area and year.

title: Methods
text:
4 Measures

4.1 Scale Scores
Student achievement is measured using scale scores in Reading and Mathematics. Scale scores range from NA to NA across the dataset.


4.2 Achievement Levels
Scale scores are classified into performance levels using grade-specific cut points. The levels in the dataset are: Advanced, No Score, Partially Proficient, Proficient, Unsatisfactory. Students at the Proficient or Advanced level are considered to have met grade-level expectations.


4.3 Organizational Variables
Records include district identifiers (DISTRICT_NUMBER) and school identifiers (SCHOOL_NUMBER), enabling analyses of between-district and between-school variation.

title: Methods
text:
5 Analytic Approach
The analyses are organized to address each research question:
RQ1 — Achievement Trends: Longitudinal achievement patterns are examined by computing mean scores and proficiency rates by year and content area using the analyzeAssessment("achievement_trends") function.
RQ2 — District Variation: Between-district comparisons use analyzeAssessment("district_comparison") to examine variation in mean scores, proficiency rates, and sample sizes across the 3 districts in the dataset.
RQ3 — Cohort Progress: Cohort tracking via analyzeAssessment("cohort_progress") examines how students with longitudinal records progress through grade levels over time.
Additionally, score distribution analyses using analyzeAssessment("score_distribution") provide percentile breakpoints (10th, 25th, 50th, 75th, 90th) to characterize the full shape of the achievement distribution.

title: Methods
text:
6 Reproducibility
All analyses are implemented in the dissertation R package and can be replicated by installing the package and calling the analysis functions directly:

library(dissertation)
summarizeAssessment(content_area = "READING", group_by = "GRADE")
analyzeAssessment("achievement_trends", content_area = "MATHEMATICS")

The complete analysis code, data, and documentation are available via the package’s GitHub repository and REST API.

title: Customization Guide
text: This guide walks through the steps to transform this template into your own dissertation.

title: Customization Guide
text: This guide walks through the steps to transform this template into your own dissertation.

title: Customization Guide
text:
Step 1: Replace the Dataset
The seed dataset (assessmentData) demonstrates the structure. Replace it with your own:
library(data.table)

# Load your data
my_data <- fread("path/to/your/data.csv")

# Save in the package format
save(my_data, file = "data/my_data.rda", compress = "xz")
Then update R/data.R with documentation matching your variables.

title: Customization Guide
text:
Step 2: Modify the Analysis Functions
Edit R/analysis.R to match your research questions. The key pattern to maintain is:
myAnalysis <- function(param1 = "default", include_meta = FALSE) {
    # 1. Validate inputs
    # 2. Filter/process data
    # 3. Compute results as a data.table
    # 4. Return structured list with status, results, interpretation
    list(
        status = "success",
        results = my_results_dt,
        interpretation = "Plain-language summary...",
        filters = list(param1 = param1)
    )
}
This pattern ensures your functions work seamlessly with the REST API and MCP tools.

title: Customization Guide
text:
Step 3: Update the API Endpoints
Edit R/api.R to expose your new functions. Follow the existing endpoint pattern:

Parse query parameters from the request
Call your analysis function
Return JSON response with CORS headers

title: Customization Guide
text:
Step 4: Write Your Dissertation Chapters
Replace the template content in quarto_website/content/chapters/ with your own writing. The Quarto files support:

Inline R code: `r nrow(assessmentData)` for dynamic values
Code chunks for tables and figures
Cross-references between sections
Bibliography via references.bib

title: Customization Guide
section: Step 5: Configure Your Three Output Formats
text:
Step 5: Configure Your Three Output Formats
The dissertation renders three ways from the same .qmd source files:

5a. Website (default)
The interactive website with navigation, search, and chapter links. No configuration needed — this is the default render:
cd quarto_website
quarto render


5b. Thesis PDF — Formal University Submission
This uses your institution’s thesis.cls to produce a PDF that meets Graduate School formatting requirements. The CU Boulder thesis.cls is included as an exemplar.
To use the included CU Boulder template:

Edit inst/thesis/thesis-preamble.tex with your dissertation metadata (title, name, committee, abstract, degree, department)
Render with:

cd quarto_website
quarto render --profile thesis
To adapt for your own university:

Obtain your institution’s thesis/dissertation .cls or .sty file. Many graduate schools provide this as a LaTeX template, or you may need to create one from a Word template (AI can help with this).
Place it in inst/thesis/, replacing (or alongside) thesis.cls
Read the annotated comments in the existing thesis.cls — they explain what each section does and what to change. The inst/thesis/ANNOTATION_GUIDE.txt provides a detailed adaptation workflow.
Edit inst/thesis/thesis-preamble.tex to use the preamble commands your class file expects
Update _quarto-thesis.yml to reference your class file:

format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: your-thesis-class
    classoption: [your, options]
    include-in-header: ../inst/thesis/thesis-preamble.tex


5c. Working Paper — Versioned HTML + PDF
A clean, modern document format for sharing and iterating on the research beyond the formal dissertation. Think of it as the dissertation becoming v1.0 of an evolving research product:
cd quarto_website
quarto render --profile working-paper
This produces both HTML and PDF with:

A versioned header (e.g., “Working Paper v1.0”)
Academic working paper styling (serif body text, clean tables, code folding)
Table of contents and section numbering
Suitable for posting to SSRN, sharing with collaborators, or hosting independently

To update the version number, edit the header in _quarto-working-paper.yml:
include-in-header: |
  \fancyhead[L]{\small\itshape Working Paper v1.1}

title: Customization Guide
text:
Step 6: Update the MCP Schema
If you modify the API endpoints, update the mcp-schema.json in the dissertation-ai repository to reflect your new tools.

title: Customization Guide
text:
Step 7: Update Package Metadata
Edit DESCRIPTION with your name, title, and description. Update R/dissertation-package.R and R/zzz.R with your package name if you rename it.

title: Customization Guide
text:
Testing Your Changes
After modifications, verify everything works:
# Run tests
devtools::test()

# Check package
devtools::check()

# Test API
run_dissertation_api()
# Visit http://localhost:8000/openapi.json
Render all three outputs to verify:
cd quarto_website
quarto render                          # Website
quarto render --profile thesis         # Formal thesis PDF
quarto render --profile working-paper  # Working paper HTML + PDF

title: Getting Started
text:
Before using this framework, ensure you have the following installed:

R (>= 4.5): https://cran.r-project.org/
Quarto (>= 1.6): https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/
Git: https://git-scm.com/
RStudio or VS Code: Your preferred IDE
Node.js (LTS, optional): For the web application extras

title: Getting Started
text:
Before using this framework, ensure you have the following installed:

R (>= 4.5): https://cran.r-project.org/
Quarto (>= 1.6): https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/
Git: https://git-scm.com/
RStudio or VS Code: Your preferred IDE
Node.js (LTS, optional): For the web application extras

title: Getting Started
text:
Quick Start

1. Fork and Clone
Fork both repositories on GitHub, then clone them:
git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/dissertation.git dissertation-rpkg
git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/dissertation-ai.git dissertation-ai


2. Install the R Package
# From the dissertation-rpkg directory
devtools::install("dissertation")

# Load and verify
library(dissertation)
data(assessmentData)
head(assessmentData)


3. Explore the Analysis Functions
# Summary statistics
summarizeAssessment()
summarizeAssessment(content_area = "READING", group_by = "GRADE")

# Detailed analyses
analyzeAssessment("achievement_trends")
analyzeAssessment("district_comparison")
analyzeAssessment("score_distribution", grade = "5")

# Chapter information
getChapterSummary("results", include_data = TRUE)

# Discovery
getAvailableAnalyses()


4. Start the REST API
run_dissertation_api(port = 8000)

# Then visit:
# http://localhost:8000/summary?content_area=READING
# http://localhost:8000/analysis?type=achievement_trends
# http://localhost:8000/openapi.json


5. Render the Outputs
The same .qmd source files produce three different outputs:
cd dissertation-rpkg/dissertation/quarto_website

# Interactive website (default)
quarto render

# Formal thesis PDF using institutional thesis.cls
quarto render --profile thesis

# Working paper (HTML + PDF) for sharing and versioning
quarto render --profile working-paper

title: Getting Started
text:
Next Steps

Read the Customization Guide to adapt the framework for your dissertation
Explore the API Reference for endpoint documentation
Review AI Ethics in Research for responsible AI integration

title: News
text:
Initial release of the AI-native dissertation framework.

Assessment dataset with 368,301 longitudinal records across 3 academic years
Core analysis functions: summarizeAssessment(), analyzeAssessment(), getChapterSummary(), getAvailableAnalyses()
Four analysis types: achievement trends, score distributions, district comparisons, cohort progress
REST API with 6 endpoints via RestRserve
Quarto website with 5 dissertation chapters
Thesis PDF output profile for institutional formatting
Comprehensive test suite
Customization guide for dissertation authors
